The destruction of Judah and Jerusalem happened gradually. The ways God uses to call back sinners through his word, ministers, conscience, and events are all signs of his compassion for them and his desire that none should perish. Notice here the terrible damage sin causes, and as we value the comfort and continuation of our earthly blessings, let us keep it from the root of those blessings. They had many times plowed and sown their land in the seventh year, when it should have rested, and now it lay unplowed and unsown for ten times seven years. God will not lose his glory ultimately because of people’s disobedience. If they refused to let the land rest, God would make it rest. What place, O God, will your justice spare if Jerusalem has perished? If that delight of yours were cut off for wickedness, let us not be proud, but fear.

God had promised the restoration of the captives and the rebuilding of Jerusalem at the end of seventy years; and that time to favor Zion, that appointed time, arrived finally. Although God’s church is cast down, it is not abandoned; although his people are corrected, they are not left behind; although thrown into the furnace, they are not lost there, nor left there any longer than until the impurities are removed. Although God contends for a long time, he will not contend forever. Before we close the books of Chronicles, which contain an accurate record of events, consider what desolation sin brought into the world, even into God’s church. Let us tremble at what is recorded here, while in the character of some few gracious souls, we see that the Lord did not leave himself without witness. And when we have looked at this faithful portrait of people by nature, let us contrast it with that same nature when recovered by Almighty grace through the justifying and soul-adorning righteousness of Christ our Savior.
